Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty loose. I'd usually try to carve out time in the evenings after work, maybe around three hours a day when I could manage it. Being 27 and working with limited resources, I couldn't always stick to a perfect schedule. I found listening to lecture recordings worked best for me, so I'd often have them playing while doing other things around my small apartment. Some weeks I'd be more disciplined, other weeks everything felt like too much effort.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was happening in my life. When deadlines were coming up, I'd cram more hours in, but otherwise I'd let things slide. With six online courses, it was hard to give consistent attention to each one. I tried to set regular times on weekends, but honestly, the motivation just wasn't always there. Life stuff kept getting in the way.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed. I wasn't one of those people who checked it daily out of habit. If I'm being honest, a lot of times I'd only log in when I felt guilty about not having done so for a while. The LMS felt more like an obligation than something I wanted to engage with.
Q4: There were brief periods where checking the LMS became somewhat routine, usually at the start of the semester when I had more energy. After a few weeks though, that routine kind of fell apart. The mindfulness intervention they had us do helped a bit with my stress, but it didn't really change my LMS habits much. I never got to the point where it felt natural or automatic to log in regularly.
Q5: The video lectures were definitely the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I'd often play them at 1.5x speed and just absorb what I could. The discussion forums were okay when people actually responded to my posts. Having 15 posts there shows I tried to participate, but it never felt like the most efficient use of my time. I appreciated when instructors recorded clear audio explanations rather than just posting text.
Q6: I pretty much ignored a lot of the supplementary reading materials and optional resources. With limited internet and resources, I had to be selective about what I downloaded and engaged with. The lengthy PDF documents felt overwhelming, and I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but never did. Some of the interactive activities seemed designed for a different kind of learner. I focused on what I absolutely needed to pass.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on the exam or in the assignments. If I could hear how it would help me practically, I was more likely to engage. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade got pushed aside. I also valued things I could consume passively, like audio content, since my energy levels were often low.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. Required ones I'd force myself to do even when I didn't want to, but optional stuff just fell off my radar completely. There's something about knowing something counts toward your grade that makes you push through the sadness and low motivation. Optional things needed more internal drive that I just didn't have most of the time.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them offline on my phone during my commute or while doing chores. I couldn't always afford stable internet, so offline studying was essential for me. I'd also take handwritten notes from what I heard and review those instead of going back to the LMS. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many times I was reviewing my handwritten notes or re-listening to downloaded audio files that wouldn't show up anywhere in the system. I'd sometimes discuss course concepts with a coworker who had taken similar classes, and that helped clarify things. The LMS would just show I hadn't logged in for days, but I might have spent hours studying from my notes. My exam score of 56 doesn't fully reflect the effort I put in offline.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download a video or audio file once, and then that would be my study material for the whole week. Going back to the LMS to re-access something felt inefficient when I already had it saved. My task completion rate looks low partly because I was working from downloaded materials rather than clicking through things online. It was just more practical given my situation.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging felt nearly impossible. When I was feeling particularly sad or down, even logging in felt like climbing a mountain. Around midterms, everything piled up and I just couldn't keep up with all six courses. My attendance dropped to around 68% partly because of these difficult stretches. The mindfulness exercises helped a little, but they couldn't fix the underlying heaviness I felt.
Q13: Absolutely. Fatigue was a big one for me—being 27 and juggling multiple responsibilities, I often came to studying already exhausted. When I was confused about material, I'd sometimes just give up rather than seek help because it felt like too much effort. Competing responsibilities from work and life meant the LMS often got whatever energy I had left, which wasn't much. My low resources made everything harder too.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd realize I'd watched twenty minutes without absorbing anything because I was too preoccupied with feeling down. Sometimes I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Those sessions felt empty even though technically I was 'engaged' with the platform.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. My assignment completion rate of 1.4% reflects that pretty clearly. Part of it was low motivation—when you're feeling sad, everything seems less urgent and less meaningful. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same heaviness. The gap between knowing something matters and actually doing it felt impossible to bridge sometimes.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to act. Without them, I probably would have engaged even less than I did. I'd usually have a burst of activity right before something was due, cramming whatever I could. The LMS became more important to me in those final hours before a deadline. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my low motivation.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When it was positive or constructive, it gave me a small boost, but that feeling never lasted long. Negative feedback sometimes made me want to disengage even more because it confirmed my fears that I wasn't doing well enough. With my assignment completion being so low, I didn't get much feedback to begin with. I think more encouraging feedback earlier might have helped me stay more engaged.
Q18: The course structure felt somewhat rigid and didn't accommodate my learning style well. I'm an auditory learner, so courses that were heavy on reading and text-based activities were harder for me to engage with. Having six online courses meant I was constantly switching between different structures and expectations. When a course had clear, predictable patterns, I could manage better, but complexity just added to my overwhelm.
Q19: Courses that front-loaded audio and video content made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks spread throughout—my task completion of 30.6% shows I struggled with that. Discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, even though I did participate in 15 posts. The lack of flexibility in deadlines was tough when I was going through harder emotional periods.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need without finding it, or they're anxious and keep checking things without actually completing work. For me, high activity might mean I was confused and looking for answers, not that I was productively engaged. Some people might also use the LMS as a form of procrastination—feeling like they're doing something without actually doing the hard work of completing assignments.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam score of 56 and passing grade came despite relatively low LMS activity because I did a lot of studying offline. Listening to downloaded lectures and reviewing my notes doesn't show up in any log. Someone might look at my login frequency and think I wasn't trying, but I was putting in about 21 hours a week of study time. The system just couldn't see most of it.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and how often you access certain materials. But they miss the quality of that engagement completely. They can't tell if I was actually absorbing information or just staring blankly at a screen feeling sad. They also miss all the offline work—the note-taking, the re-listening, the thinking about concepts while doing other things. It's like looking at footprints without knowing the journey.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's when the material sticks with you beyond just passing the exam. For me, true engagement happened in those moments when I was genuinely curious about something, even if those moments were rare this semester. It's not about how many times you click on something—it's about whether it changes how you think or what you know.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally throughout this course. It wouldn't capture the sadness that made every task feel heavier than it should. It also wouldn't show the offline studying that actually got me through, or the peer conversations that helped clarify concepts. My low engagement numbers don't reflect someone who didn't care—they reflect someone who was fighting through a fog just to keep going. The mindfulness intervention helped a bit, but that internal struggle is invisible in any log.
